---
title: "How to ask for help"
date: "2026-08-24T09:02"
categories: [advice]
---

When working through the examples or exercises, you will get stuck. It's inevitable.

**You absolutely can (and should) reach out when stuck.** E-mail is the best way to contact me, and I generally respond to course-related messages within 24–48 hours on weekdays. No question is too tricky or embarrassing, I promise. Computers are extraordinarily literal and tiny typos will often mess you up for a long time—I've lost countless time because of missing commas and misspelled words (curse you `lenght()`).

This class is different from most of your other past classes. In many classes, you can work through the readings and materials without ever interacting with your classmates or professor—you can just follow the class schedule, get the assignments done, and finish it all.

While you can theoretically do that here (and some students to), **I do not recommend it**. [As I talk about here, building a class community is crucial for success in this course.](2026-01-05_building-community.qmd)

You can (and should!) ask questions when you get stuck. E-mail me, or ask a classmate if you are working together. Don't feel bad about asking for help!


### The 30-minute rule

I’m a big believer in the 30-minute rule: 

::: {.callout-tip}
#### The 30-minute rule

If you're stuck on an issue for more than 30 minutes, **stop**, take a break, and ask for help somewhere.
:::

There are a few easy guidelines to remember when asking for help:


### Don't suffer in silence!

*Do not suffer in silence*. When you're stuck, reach out. Do not wallow in your stuck-ness.

I've had past students tell me that's like the one thing they'll remember from my classes—do not suffer in silence. I mean it, and I'll keep saying it throughout the semester (because often in your past courses and degrees, you've been discouraged from reaching out or from building communities or whatever—that is *not* the case here).


### Share what you learn

Lots of you will e-mail me for assistance, and that's great—I'm happy to respond. Many students will have similar questions, so I may turn recurring questions into anonymized course tips or FAQs that everyone can use.

You can help classmates too. Some of you have R experience already, and even if you don't, as the semester goes on, you'll get more comfortable with it and will start being able to answer one another's questions. You might have just fixed a similar issue in a past exercise, or you might be able to spot a typo in someone else's code. If you are studying together and know how to help, step in!

You might feel apprehensive about asking for help. Don't worry: I can assure you that there are no dumb R questions. If you have a question about something, someone else probably does too!


### Help us help you

Ask questions with as complete information as possible. Do not just post a photo from your phone and say something like "help?" or "my code isn't working" and that's it—provide more background (it's hard to read your computer's mind). 

Explain specifically what you're trying to do and provide code when possible. In an e-mail or other Markdown-formatted message, you can format R code by putting it between triple backticks like this:

````default
```
ggplot(blah) + geom_point()
```
````

If you add "r" after the first set of triple backticks it will add R-based syntax highlighting too (you can use other languages like "python" or "sql" or whatever too, but you don't need to worry about that for this class):

````default
```r
ggplot(blah) + geom_point()
```
````

You can also take screenshots to *supplement* your question (use ⌘+shift+4 on macOS to save a screenshot to your Desktop, or ⌘+⌥+shift+4 to save a screenshot directly to the clipboard; use Windows+shift+S on Windows to save a screenshot directly to the clipboard). **Do not send a screenshot as your entire question though** (it's very tempting to just send a picture of an error and hope that someone can fix it)—provide context. Screenshots are helpful, but code/text is better because people can't copy and paste code from an image into RStudio to troubleshoot it.

Try making your question a **repr**oducible **ex**ample (or reprex). You'll learn more about reprexes in session 8, but it's an incredibly important skill to have. [See here for some resources about it](../content/08-content.qmd#reproducible-examples)


### High resolution questions

Relatedly, embrace [high resolution writing](https://chriscoyier.net/2022/12/28/watch-out-for-low-resolution-writing/) (vs. low resolution writing), when asking questions, so that your messages contain a lot of detail and context.

For instance, here's an example of **low resolution** question-asking:

:::: {.callout-note icon=false}
#### Low-resolution help request

**Person A:** I'm stuck on Exercise X

**Person B:** Ok cool—what's the problem you're having?

::: {.text-center}
*(10 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** My code isn't running

**Person B:** What does your code look like?

::: {.text-center}
*(10 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** It's giving me an error

**Person A:** ggplot() + geom_point()

**Person B:** What is the error? What do you have in your code before that one line?

::: {.text-center}
*(20 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** [*Phone photo of part of the screen*]

**Person B:** It might be an issue with X or Y, but it's hard to see…

::: {.text-center}
*(10 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** [*Phone photo of a different part of the screen*]

::: {.text-center}
(*…and so on until the issue is figured out*)
:::
::::

Phew. That process takes forever and is incredibly slow.

Here's a **high resolution** version of the same situation:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
#### High-resolution help request

**Person A:** I'm stuck on Exercise X. For some reason, my code isn't running and it's giving me an error that says `Error in ggplot(): could not find function "ggplot"`. It happens after I run this code:

```r
ggplot(data = mpg, aes(x = hwy, y = displ)) + 
  geom_point()
```

Earlier in the document I have this code: `library(tidyverse)`, but when I run that, I get this error: `Error in library(): there is no package called tidyverse’`. 

Does anyone know what might be going on?

**Person B:** Ah cool, that's an easy fix! That just means that you don't have the tidyverse library installed on your computer. You can fix it by either using the Packages panel in RStudio to install it, or run this in your console:

```r
install.packages("tidyverse")
```

↑ That should fix things

::: {.text-center}
*(10 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** That worked—thanks!
:::

That second example is much longer than just saying "my code isn't working", but it gets an answer *significantly* faster because it contains complete details to help someone else help.

**Embrace high resolution writing.**


### Unanswered questions

Stuff like this happens all the time on websites where people ask for help:

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
#### An incomplete help request

**Person A:** I'm stuck with this issue and can't get X to work

::: {.text-center}
*(10 minutes later…)*
:::

**Person A:** Oh wait, never mind, I fixed it!

::: {.text-center}
*(Person A disappears…)*
:::
:::

If you ask a question and then figure out the answer yourself, **let us know how you did it!** It's likely that other people have similar questions and issues, and seeing solved-but-unexplained questions is sad:

![[xkcd #979: "Wisdom of the Ancients"](https://xkcd.com/979/)](img/wisdom_of_the_ancients.png)


### Help-request etiquette

Whether you e-mail me or ask someone else for help, follow a few general guidelines:

1. **Use an informative subject**: Don't use only "Help!", "Homework question", or "Stuck!" as your e-mail subject. Similar to the idea of [high-resolution question asking](#high-resolution-questions), make it more informative, such as "Can't change color in plot in exercise 3."
2. **Keep each request focused**: Include the context needed for one issue. If you have a different question or topic, start a new e-mail with a clear subject so the request is easy to follow.
3. **Follow up when you solve it**: Similar to the idea of [unanswered questions](#unanswered-questions), if you figure out the answer yourself, reply with a short explanation of what fixed it. That confirms the issue is resolved and may help with similar questions later.


### Student appointments

You can meet with me for [student hours](../syllabus.qmd#student-hours) by contacting me by e-mail to arrange a mutually convenient time.
